content:
Hi! Recently in a patch review that I received (the patch review from Alexander on my shmem series in particular) and I noticed that some of the suggested code actually used unwrap() vs. unwrap_unchecked():

// PANIC: `populate` has just succeeded, guaranteeing that `sgt_res` is populated.
Ok(sgt_res.as_ref().unwrap())

Where previously this would have used unwrap_unchecked(), in part because the actual assignment that populated the variable was on the line directly above the unchecked unwrap.

Do we have an explicit rule for when to use one or the other?

FWIW, I've always preferred unwrap_unchecked() for locations where the actual Some() assignment is guaranteed by a type invariant, or the assignment happens immediately above the unchecked unwrap in the source file. And I've usually explicitly avoided unwrap(). I can see some situations where I would possibly want to use unwrap() if I expect it to be possible that a type invariant could be violated on the C side, but that's about it.
Option::insert can give you a reference possible, but I suppose you need a immutable reference here?

Well I mean of course, I try to avoid both when possible - but it does come up quite often when doing stuff like making guarantees around how C apis work. I'm mostly wondering as a general rule once you've tried other things
If you have to choose between two, and if you know that compiler can see the Option not being able to be None -- then use unwrap() with panic comment would be my preferred option
If there's no code size penalty then there's not really down side of unwrap() compared to unwrap_unchecked()
Oh! I didn't even realize the compiler could optimize out unwrap()s
Hmm, I just discovered a case where Rust compiler failed to optimize this, when the type has a niche. https://godbolt.org/z/d5Ybzdzdx
When there's a niche, the Some tag write becomes implicit and LLVM apparently does know about this anymore.
So I withdraw what I said; DevRes has a niche, so unwrap_unchecked() would be preferred
